program: Travel English -- Europe Without a Translator
hub_subtitle: Travel English for the airport, the check-in counter, the hotel and passport control — so that Europe needs no translator

lesson.menu_title: What Would You Like to Order? -- Dinner in Porto
lesson.menu_desc: Asking politely with would like, reading a real menu, comparing two dishes, and paying the bill -- alone -- 38 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 6 -- Eating Out in Europe
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rosângela | Lesson 6 -- Eating Out in Europe

listenings.text: Good evening, and welcome to A Cozinha do Porto. My name is Nuno, and I am your waiter tonight. Here is the menu. Before you order, let me tell you about our specials. For the appetizer, I recommend the tomato soup. It is very light, and it is smaller than the cheese plate, so you still have room for the main course. For the main course, the grilled fish is our special tonight. It is fresh, it comes from the sea this morning, and it is cheaper than the beef. The beef is bigger, and it is our most popular dish, but it is heavier. Would you like still water or sparkling water with your meal? And for dessert, we have the chocolate cake and the orange tart. The tart is lighter than the cake, and after a big main course, many people would like something light. One more thing, and this is important: if you are allergic to something, please tell me now, before you order. We can change the dish for you, and the kitchen is very careful. I am coming back in five minutes. Take your time with the menu.

Hello. My name is Marta, and I am at the next table, with my sister. Is this your first time here? The food is very good. Can I tell you something? Twenty years ago, I never opened a menu in a restaurant. I used to wait for my husband, because he always ordered for me. I used to say, the same, please, and that was all. It is true! But then I started traveling, and everything changed. Now I read the menu, I ask the waiter what he recommends, and I order what I want. I used to be afraid of the bill, too. In Portugal the bill is different, and the tip is smaller than in the United States. Here you leave five or ten percent, and it is enough. Nobody is angry. And the food? I used to eat only meat. I never ate fish, never. Now I love it: the fish here is fresher than at home, and it is cheaper than the meat. My sister says I am a different person now. She is right. Look, your waiter is coming. Enjoy your dinner!

paras: It is eight o'clock in Porto, and Rosângela is sitting at a table by the window, with her husband. The restaurant is full, and it is louder than she expected. A waiter is coming to the table with two menus. "Good evening. Would you like something to drink?" Her husband is looking at Rosângela, and he is not saying anything, because he does not speak English -- and tonight he does not need to. "Good evening," she says. "We would like a bottle of sparkling water, please."

The menu is long, and Rosângela is reading it slowly. She knows the words now: appetizer, main course, dessert. "What do you recommend?" she asks. The waiter smiles. "The grilled fish is our special tonight. It is fresher than the meat, and it is cheaper." "Perfect. We would like two tomato soups for the appetizer, and then the grilled fish for two." Then she stops, and she says the sentence she practiced at home, slowly and clearly: "One more thing. My husband is allergic to nuts. Is there anything with nuts in the fish?" The waiter goes to the kitchen, and he comes back in one minute. "No nuts. It is safe."

The fish is excellent. Her husband eats everything, and he says nothing -- but he is smiling. For dessert, Rosângela would like the orange tart, because it is lighter than the chocolate cake, and after a big main course she does not want something heavy. At the end, she raises her hand: "Could we have the bill, please?" The bill comes on a small plate. She reads it, and she leaves a tip -- ten percent, the normal tip in Portugal. Outside, on the street, her husband takes her hand. "You did everything," he says, in Portuguese. And it is true. She used to be the person who waited at the table. Tonight she is the person who ordered.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Rosângela does not like the food in Porto, and she is asking the waiter to change the dish.
Rosângela runs the whole dinner alone -- she orders, she asks about the allergy, and she asks for the bill.
Rosângela's husband is ordering for both of them, because the menu is only in English.

items: Rosângela orders sparkling water for the table.
She says: "We would like a bottle of sparkling water, please."
The waiter recommends the meat, because it is fresher than the fish.
He recommends the grilled fish: it is fresher than the meat, and it is cheaper.
Rosângela tells the waiter that her husband is allergic to nuts.
She says: "My husband is allergic to nuts. Is there anything with nuts in the fish?"
Rosângela chooses the chocolate cake, because it is lighter than the orange tart.
She would like the orange tart, because the tart is lighter than the cake.
Rosângela's husband asks for the bill and leaves the tip.
She asks: "Could we have the bill, please?" And she leaves the tip -- ten percent.

items.situation: You arrive at the restaurant with your husband. There is a free table by the window. Ask the waiter for it.
tips: Good evening. We would like a table for two, please.
Could we have the table by the window?

items.situation: "Would you like something to drink?" You do not want water with gas. Answer.
tips: We would like a bottle of still water, please.
Still water for me, and sparkling water for my husband.

items.situation: The menu is long and you do not know the dishes. Ask the waiter for help.
You want the tomato soup first, and then the grilled fish. Order for two people.
tips: We would like two tomato soups for the appetizer, please.
And then the grilled fish for two.

items.situation: Your husband cannot eat nuts. Tell the waiter before you order.
tips: My husband is allergic to nuts.
Is there anything with nuts in this dish?

items.situation: The waiter offers the chocolate cake and the orange tart. You want the lighter one. Say it, and say why.
tips: I would like the orange tart, please.
The tart is lighter than the cake, and the main course was big.

items.situation: Dinner is finished and you want to pay. Call the waiter politely.
tips: Excuse me. Could we have the bill, please?
We would like to pay, please.

items.situation: "Do you accept cards?" is not the question -- the waiter asks if everything was good. Answer, and say what you liked.
tips: The fish was better than I expected.
